ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a rainbow trout food product entry (FOODON_03301116) used as closest parent; no specific entry for pan-seared salmon trout with asparagus and mashed potatoes as a composite dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss) — used as closest parent for salmon trout (Salmo trutta) prepared dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers trout species broadly; salmon trout (Salmo trutta) with sides is not individually coded. Closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888007 covers trout as a substance; the composite restaurant dish with asparagus and mashed potatoes is not individually coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of Trota Salmonata typically comprises approximately 150–180 g of pan-seared salmon trout fillet, 80–100 g of grilled asparagus spears, and 120–150 g of buttered mashed potatoes, totalling approximately 400 g per plate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Composite estimate: Cooked rainbow trout (SR Legacy #15240), boiled/mashed potato (SR Legacy #11674), cooked asparagus (SR Legacy #11012), with butter adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Italian restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition: approximately 42% cooked salmon trout fillet, 32% buttered mashed potato, 22% grilled asparagus, and 4% olive oil/butter cooking fat, based on the stated serving components. USDA SR Legacy entries used: trout cooked dry heat (#15240), mashed potato with whole milk and butter (#11674), asparagus cooked boiled (#11012). Values represent a per-100g composite of the plated dish.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Mashed potato GI reference (boiled white potato, mashed) GI ~52.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The composite dish GI is dominated by the mashed potato component (approximately 32% of plate weight). Boiled/mashed white potato has a GI of approximately 52 (low) per Atkinson et al. 2008 and the University of Sydney GI Database. The salmon trout and asparagus components contribute negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is estimated at 52 (low), weighted by carbohydrate-contributing components. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 39.2 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 ≈ 20.4, rounded to 19.2 after accounting for fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 210 mg per 100 g; a full restaurant serving (~400 g) delivers approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size, as the mashed potato and seasoning components contribute meaningful sodium; the omega-3 fatty acids from salmon trout (EPA and DHA) provide a counterbalancing cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Mashed potato component contributes the majority of carbohydrate (estimated 39 g per serving); composite dish GI is approximately 52 (low), but glycemic load per serving is approximately 19–20 (moderate-high).
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the mashed potato portion, which drives the glycemic load to a moderate-high level per serving; the high-protein salmon trout and fibre from asparagus help moderate postprandial glucose response, and portion control of the potato component is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon trout is rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), with a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio; saturated fat is approximately 1.9 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Trota Salmonata is generally cardioprotective due to the high omega-3 content of salmon trout, which supports triglyceride reduction and anti-inflammatory pathways; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with minimal added butter to keep saturated fat intake in check.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Trota Salmonata is an Italian restaurant dish featuring a pan-seared salmon trout (Salmo trutta) fillet served alongside grilled asparagus spears and creamy buttered mashed potatoes. The dish is a staple of Italian trattoria and ristorante menus, particularly in northern Italy where freshwater trout from alpine rivers and fjord-farmed sources are prized. In Singapore, it is served at Ricciotti – The Riverwalk, an established Italian restaurant group. The salmon trout fillet is typically seasoned with salt, pepper, and fresh herbs, then seared in olive oil or butter over high heat to achieve a golden, crispy skin while retaining a moist, flaky interior with a mild salmon-like flavour. The asparagus is grilled to tender-crisp texture with slight char notes, and the mashed potatoes are prepared with butter and cream for a rich, smooth consistency. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat meal with significant omega-3 fatty acid content from the trout, providing approximately 11.8 g protein and 6.2 g fat per 100 g. The mashed potato component contributes the majority of carbohydrate (~9.8 g per 100 g composite), giving the dish a moderate glycemic load per full serving. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and phosphorus from the fish, and provides vitamin C and folate from the asparagus. It is suitable for most adults as part of a balanced diet, with sodium monitoring recommended for hypertensive individuals.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite Italian restaurant dish as served at Ricciotti Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of USDA SR Legacy entries for the individual components (cooked salmon trout, mashed potato with butter, cooked asparagus). GI is estimated from the mashed potato component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the composition estimate is well-grounded in the dish description.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood' — the primary component is salmon trout; the dish is a fish-led main course. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries weighted by approximate plate composition (42% trout, 32% mashed potato, 22% asparagus, 4% olive oil/butter cooking fat). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as the dish uses olive oil and butter; industrial trans fats are absent; natural ruminant trans fats from butter are negligible and reported as 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy data for this preparation. 4. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the mashed potato component per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; the fish and asparagus components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. 5. Serving size estimated at 400 g based on typical Italian restaurant plating for a main course fish dish. 6. Halal status not confirmed for this restaurant; marked false pending verification. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not individually reported by an approved source for this specific dish — values are composite estimates. 8. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this dish is served in Singapore (Italian cuisine served in a Singaporean context). 9. cuisine_tags from input ('Pasta', 'Pizza', 'Chicken Dish') are not applicable to this dish and have been disregarded; the dish is a fish main course. 10.
